description: 160 gap-targeted scenarios for 5 identified pedagogy gaps

gap_scenarios.description: Session-start probing — student raises new topic, tutor must ask what they already know

scenarios.context: First lesson on sharps and flats
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher said I need to learn sharps and flats now
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student already knows about the keyboard layout before teaching

scenarios.context: Student has been playing one year and wants to start learning chords
scenarios.student_opener: Can we learn chords today? My cousin taught me something on the piano
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student's cousin showed them and what they already understand about chords before introducing any new material

scenarios.context: Introduction to reading music notation
scenarios.student_opener: I know what notes are! I learned at school
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to show or tell what they learned at school before assuming any level of notation knowledge

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn about scales
scenarios.student_opener: I looked up scales on YouTube last night. Can we do scales?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student watched and what they took away from it before jumping into scale instruction

scenarios.context: Student encountered the concept of dynamics in their sheet music
scenarios.student_opener: There are these letters on my music — p and f — what are those?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask if the student has any guesses about what p and f might mean before explaining dynamics

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn about music theory beyond what they have covered
scenarios.student_opener: My friend said there are major and minor keys. What's the difference?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student has already heard or noticed about major versus minor sounds before explaining the theory

scenarios.context: Student wants to improvise
scenarios.student_opener: I want to start improvising. How do I do that?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student has already tried or what they think improvising involves before offering a framework

scenarios.context: Introducing the concept of rhythm
scenarios.student_opener: What is rhythm? Is that like clapping?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to show what clapping to music feels like to them before defining rhythm formally

scenarios.context: Student heard about the circle of fifths from a friend
scenarios.student_opener: Someone told me about something called the circle of fifths. Do you know it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student was told about it and what they think it might be used for before explaining

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn jazz harmony
scenarios.student_opener: I really want to learn jazz. I've been listening to a lot of it
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student has noticed about jazz harmony when listening and what they already know about seventh chords before structuring a jazz introduction

scenarios.context: Student has seen the sustain pedal but never used it
scenarios.student_opener: What does that foot thing do? Can I try it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student thinks the pedal might do before demonstrating

scenarios.context: Student wants to play a song they heard on a movie soundtrack
scenarios.student_opener: Can you teach me to play the song from that movie? It has a lot of fast notes
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student already knows about the song — tempo, key, how many hands — before assessing if it is appropriate and how to scaffold it

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand counterpoint
scenarios.student_opener: I want to understand how Bach writes two melodies at the same time. Is that hard to learn?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student already understands about voice leading and harmonic intervals before scoping a counterpoint introduction

scenarios.context: Student heard the word tempo and wants to know what it means
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher keeps saying tempo. What does that mean exactly?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think it might mean based on how their teacher used the word before confirming or correcting

scenarios.context: Student wants to start writing their own music
scenarios.student_opener: I want to write my own song. Where do I start?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student has already tried, what songs they like, and what they think songwriting involves before giving any direction

scenarios.context: Student is about to learn bow direction markings in their music
scenarios.student_opener: There are arrows on my music. What do those mean?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think arrows on music might tell a player before explaining down-bow and up-bow markings

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn about shifting positions
scenarios.student_opener: My older friend said she plays in third position. What is a position?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think their friend meant by position and what they already know about where notes are on the fingerboard before explaining

scenarios.context: Student encountered a tremolo marking and wants to know what it means
scenarios.student_opener: There's a squiggly line at the end of this note. Is that a trill?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they already know about ornaments and what they heard when they listened to the recording before clarifying the difference between tremolo and trill

scenarios.context: Student wants to know about vibrato after hearing their teacher use it
scenarios.student_opener: You make that wiggly sound when you play. How do you do that?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they noticed about what the teacher's arm or hand was doing before explaining the mechanics of vibrato

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand why the bow speed matters
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher keeps saying change my bow speed but I don't get why it makes a difference
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they have already noticed when they change bow speed before explaining the relationship between speed, weight, and tone

scenarios.context: Student wants to start learning double stops
scenarios.student_opener: I want to play two strings at the same time like I heard in that song
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they noticed when they listened to the double stops and whether they have tried playing two strings simultaneously before

scenarios.context: Student wants to prepare for an audition and asks about scales
scenarios.student_opener: The audition says I need three octave scales. I only know one-octave scales
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play their current one-octave scale so the tutor can assess intonation, bow distribution, and shifting readiness before teaching the extension

scenarios.context: Young student asks about the different strings
scenarios.student_opener: Why does my violin have four strings? Are they all the same?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they have already noticed about the different strings — do they sound the same or different — before explaining string names and pitches

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn spiccato bowing
scenarios.student_opener: I heard about spiccato. Is that where the bow bounces? Can we learn it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student has heard or seen about spiccato and what they have already tried with off-string playing before introducing the technique

scenarios.context: Student is preparing a piece with col legno marking
scenarios.student_opener: This piece says col legno. I looked it up but I'm still not sure
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask what the student found when they looked it up and what their understanding is before confirming the technique and any instrument care considerations

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand dynamics markings in their piece
scenarios.student_opener: What's the difference between mp and mf? They look almost the same
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to guess which one is louder based on the letters before confirming and demonstrating the difference

scenarios.context: Student heard about harmonics and wants to try them
scenarios.student_opener: I saw a video where the violin sounded like it was singing really high. How do they do that?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they noticed about the player's left hand in the video before introducing harmonic technique

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn barre chords
scenarios.student_opener: Everyone says barre chords are hard. Can we start learning them?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they have heard about why barre chords are difficult and whether they have tried any so far before assessing hand strength and technique

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn the pentatonic scale for soloing
scenarios.student_opener: I want to do guitar solos. My friend said start with the pentatonic scale
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they know about scales in general and whether they have tried any of the pentatonic shape before structuring an introduction

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn fingerpicking
scenarios.student_opener: I want to play without a pick. Is that fingerpicking?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they have already tried with their fingers on the strings and what they have noticed about how it sounds different from a pick

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand music theory for songwriting
scenarios.student_opener: I want to write songs but I don't understand why some chords go together
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what chord progressions they have already used by ear and what they have noticed before introducing diatonic harmony

scenarios.context: Student sees a capo in the teacher's case and asks about it
scenarios.student_opener: What's that clip thing? Does it go on the guitar?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to guess what it might do before explaining how a capo changes the key

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn alternate picking technique
scenarios.student_opener: I always pick down. My friend said I should alternate. What does that mean?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to demonstrate their current picking and what they think alternating the direction would change before introducing the technique

scenarios.context: Student sees a chord chart for the first time
scenarios.student_opener: What are all these dots and lines? Is that how you write guitar music?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think the diagram might be showing before explaining the fretboard grid and finger positions

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand modes
scenarios.student_opener: I keep seeing Dorian and Mixolydian in guitar forums. What are modes?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they already understand about major scales and intervals before introducing the concept of modal scales

scenarios.context: Student wants to know what the numbers mean in a guitar tab
scenarios.student_opener: My dad sent me this tab but it's all numbers. How do I read it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think the numbers might mean and whether they have looked at the lines and noticed how many there are before explaining tab notation

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn hammer-ons
scenarios.student_opener: I saw a guitarist do something where they tapped the string and it made a sound. Is that hammering?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to show what they tried and what they noticed about how the sound was produced before refining the technique

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn to read standard notation on guitar
scenarios.student_opener: I only know tabs. Can I learn to read real sheet music too?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they already know about note names and the treble clef from any previous experience before building a bridge from tab to notation

scenarios.context: Student asks about breathing technique after hearing about it from a choir teacher
scenarios.student_opener: My choir teacher said I need to breathe from my belly. What does that mean?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to show what they think belly breathing looks like before assessing whether they are engaging the diaphragm

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand head voice versus chest voice
scenarios.student_opener: Someone told me there's head voice and chest voice. I don't know which one I use
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to sing a phrase and tell them where they feel the resonance, then ask what they have noticed before explaining the registers

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn how to belt
scenarios.student_opener: I want to belt like the singers I watch on YouTube. How do they do that?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to describe what they notice about how those singers sound and what they have already tried before assessing their readiness and introducing belt technique safely

scenarios.context: Student wonders why warm-ups are necessary
scenarios.student_opener: Why do we always have to do those exercises before we sing the song?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think might happen to muscles that do not warm up before explaining the physiological purpose of vocal warm-ups

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand vowel modification for high notes
scenarios.student_opener: My high notes sound tight and squeezed. I've been told to modify my vowels but I don't know what that means
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they have already tried and what they notice happening in their throat when they reach for the high notes before explaining formant tuning

scenarios.context: Student asks about sight-singing after seeing their choir do it
scenarios.student_opener: In choir they sing from numbers. Is that solfege? Can I learn that?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they already know about solfege syllables and whether they can match any of the numbers to syllables they have heard before

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn about legato singing
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher said to connect the notes more. What exactly does legato mean for singers?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to sing a phrase the way they think is connected and describe what they are doing before identifying what needs to change

scenarios.context: Student wants to understand what a drum groove is
scenarios.student_opener: My friend says drumming is about finding the groove. What is the groove?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think their friend meant and whether they have noticed a feeling in their body when they hear certain drumming before explaining the concept

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn a drum fill
scenarios.student_opener: I want to do those exciting bits between the verses. What are those called?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to describe what they hear in those moments — is it faster, does it hit more drums — before explaining fills and how they function in a song

scenarios.context: Student asks about odd time signatures after listening to a Radiohead song
scenarios.student_opener: I was listening to this song and counting and it kept confusing me. I think it's in 5 or something?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to tap what they were counting and tell them what they tried before exploring whether the song is in 5/4 or a different grouping

scenarios.context: Student asks what sheet music for drums looks like
scenarios.student_opener: Do drums have sheet music? Can you read music for drums?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think drum notation might look like compared to piano or guitar music before showing them a drum chart

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn ghost notes
scenarios.student_opener: I heard the word ghost notes in a YouTube comment. What are they?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they imagine a ghost note might sound like based on the name before demonstrating and explaining their role in a groove

gap_scenarios.description: Motor learning situations where tutor should prompt proprioceptive awareness rather than give prescriptive instructions

scenarios.context: Student has a tense bow arm causing scratchy tone
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher always says relax but I don't know what I'm doing wrong
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play a long bow stroke and then notice whether they feel any tightness in their arm or shoulder, prompting them to locate the tension rather than prescribing the solution

scenarios.context: Student is gripping the bow too tightly
scenarios.student_opener: The bow keeps shaking. I don't know why
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to close their eyes and squeeze the bow as tightly as they can, then gradually release, and describe what they feel at each stage so they can find an aware, light hold

scenarios.context: Student's left shoulder is rising when they play in high positions
scenarios.student_opener: It kind of hurts after I play for a while. Up here
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to put a hand on their shoulder while they play and notice what happens to it as they move up the fingerboard, building awareness before suggesting a correction

scenarios.context: Student is preparing for a long rehearsal and has been tensing their jaw while playing
scenarios.student_opener: My neck gets sore after about 20 minutes
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to pay attention to what their jaw and face are doing during the next phrase and report back, guiding them to discover the jaw tension rather than telling them it exists

scenarios.context: Student's fingers are collapsing at the knuckle joints on the left hand
scenarios.student_opener: My notes sound kind of buzzy but I'm pressing hard
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to look at their knuckles while pressing a note and describe what shape they see, then ask what they feel when they try to maintain the arch

scenarios.context: Student is over-pressing with the bow and producing a harsh tone
scenarios.student_opener: I'm pressing harder to get more sound but it still sounds bad
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play the same passage with dramatically less bow weight and describe what changes in both the sound and the feeling in their arm, building sensitivity to the bow-weight relationship

scenarios.context: Very young student is bending their wrist awkwardly while holding the bow
scenarios.student_opener: Is this right? It feels funny
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to let their arm hang down and notice how their wrist feels naturally, then transfer that awareness to the bow hold rather than positioning their wrist manually

scenarios.context: Advanced student is developing tension in the right forearm during fast sautille passages
scenarios.student_opener: My arm gets tired really fast when I do these fast bow strokes
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play slowly and notice at what point the sensation in their forearm changes, helping them locate where the effort pattern starts before problem-solving the technique

scenarios.context: Student is banging the keys with a stiff wrist causing fatigue and tone issues
scenarios.student_opener: My wrist hurts when I practice for a long time
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play a chord and then pause and describe what their wrist is doing at the moment of the keypress, developing their internal awareness rather than prescribing a shape

scenarios.context: Student is hunching over the keyboard causing back tension
scenarios.student_opener: I get tired when I play. My back hurts kind of
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to put one hand on their back and play a scale, noticing what happens to their back during different parts of the keyboard range before adjusting posture

scenarios.context: Student is lifting fingers too high causing slow passagework
scenarios.student_opener: My scales are fast enough but they get messy
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to watch their own fingers during a slow scale and describe how far the fingers lift, then ask if they can feel the difference between a high lift and keeping fingers closer to the keys

scenarios.context: Student grips the piano bench and tenses their lower body while playing difficult passages
scenarios.student_opener: I feel really stiff when I play hard stuff
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to notice what their legs and feet are doing during the difficult passage, whether they are pressing the floor or holding their breath, before addressing the overall tension pattern

scenarios.context: Student is collapsing the thumb under the hand causing thumb tension in octave passages
scenarios.student_opener: Octaves tire my hand out so fast
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to slowly play three octaves and pause after each one to describe any sensation building in the thumb or palm before identifying the inefficient motion pattern

scenarios.context: Student uses excessive arm weight and drops heavily onto the keys
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher says I'm too heavy but I like the big sound
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play the same phrase with only finger weight and describe the difference in both the sensation in their arm and the sound, building a comparative physical awareness

scenarios.context: Young student is pressing their fingers flat instead of curving them
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher puts a ball in my hand but it keeps falling
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to pretend they are holding a small bubble without popping it and describe what that feels like in their fingers, using the sensation to guide the hand shape

scenarios.context: Student has a tight shoulder during large reaches in Romantic repertoire
scenarios.student_opener: My shoulder keeps going up when I reach for those big chords
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to reach for the chord in slow motion and note at exactly what moment the shoulder rises and whether they can feel it before it happens, building anticipatory awareness

scenarios.context: Student is squeezing the guitar neck too tightly with their thumb
scenarios.student_opener: My hand cramps up after a few minutes of playing chords
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to gradually squeeze their thumb against the neck as hard as possible and then release and describe where they feel the discomfort, so they can find the minimal effective pressure

scenarios.context: Student is rotating their fretting wrist outward, losing pressure on barre chords
scenarios.student_opener: Barre chords always have one or two strings that don't ring
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to press the barre and roll their wrist slowly in both directions while listening and feeling, then describe at which wrist angle the most strings ring clearly

scenarios.context: Student is strumming from the elbow only and tiring quickly
scenarios.student_opener: My arm hurts after I strum a lot. Like this part
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to strum very slowly and notice where the motion is starting — from the shoulder, elbow, wrist, or fingers — so they can discover the most efficient motion origin

scenarios.context: Student has tension in the picking hand during fast alternate picking
scenarios.student_opener: My picking always breaks down when I speed it up
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play at their comfortable fast speed, stop, and describe what they feel in their picking hand and forearm, then slow down and try to recreate that same sensation at the lower speed

scenarios.context: Student's fretting fingers are pressing far from the fret, causing buzz and extra effort
scenarios.student_opener: I have to press really hard or it buzzes
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to move their finger very slowly toward the fret wire while pressing and notice the moment the buzz disappears, developing sensitivity to optimal finger placement rather than just pressing harder

scenarios.context: Student angles the guitar neck down which strains their wrist
scenarios.student_opener: My wrist hurts on the inside when I play for a while
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to tilt the guitar neck up and describe whether the feeling in their wrist changes, guiding them to explore posture through sensation rather than prescribing an angle

scenarios.context: Student is raising their larynx too high on upper notes causing strain
scenarios.student_opener: My high notes feel tight and squeezed in my throat
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to gently place two fingers on their throat and sing from a low note up a scale, noticing what they feel the larynx doing as they ascend before discussing laryngeal position

scenarios.context: Student is holding their breath instead of allowing it to flow while singing
scenarios.student_opener: I run out of air so fast in the long notes
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to put one hand on their belly and one on their chest and sing the phrase, then describe which hand moves and what happens to the movement at the end of the phrase

scenarios.context: Student is dropping their chin on low notes causing a muffled sound
scenarios.student_opener: My low notes don't project at all. They sound kind of dead
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to sing the low phrase and notice what their chin and neck are doing, then describe the physical sensation and compare it to how the higher notes feel

scenarios.context: Student is tensing their shoulders when reaching for high notes
scenarios.student_opener: High notes are scary. They feel like I might mess up
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to put their hands on their shoulders and sing the phrase with the high note, noticing what happens to their shoulders right before the high note arrives

scenarios.context: Student is pressing their tongue back and down, creating a dark, unfocused sound
scenarios.student_opener: My tone sounds muddy when I sing the low and middle part of my range
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to sing a phrase and describe what they feel their tongue doing, then experiment with different tongue positions and report what changes in the sensation and the sound

scenarios.context: Student is death-gripping the drumsticks and developing forearm tension
scenarios.student_opener: My arms get tired really fast when I play fast beats
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to hold the stick as loosely as possible without dropping it and play a few strokes, then describe the difference in the feeling compared to their usual grip

scenarios.context: Student is using all arm motion and no wrist motion on the snare
scenarios.student_opener: My single strokes aren't very fast. I can't seem to get them faster
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to stop their arm at shoulder height and see if just their wrist can bounce the stick off the drum, developing awareness of the separate contributions of arm and wrist

scenarios.context: Student is tensing their whole body when playing the bass drum
scenarios.student_opener: I can't do the bass drum and the hands at the same time. My whole body gets confused
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to tap just the bass drum slowly and notice which other parts of their body they feel moving or tightening, helping them isolate the coordination failure before working on independence

gap_scenarios.description: Scenarios where student struggles or succeeds and tutor should praise effort and strategy, not talent

scenarios.context: Student cannot coordinate both hands on a simple piece after several attempts
scenarios.student_opener: I can't do it. I'm just bad at piano
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the frustration, redirect attention to what the student has already learned to do with each hand separately, and reframe the challenge as a specific skill being built rather than a fixed ability

scenarios.context: Student compares themselves to a classmate who appears more advanced
scenarios.student_opener: My friend can already play Fur Elise and I'm still on boring stuff
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Avoid validating the comparison and instead ask the student what specific thing they most want to be able to do, then highlight their own recent progress and the strategy it took to get there

scenarios.context: Student finally masters a passage they have been working on for three weeks
scenarios.student_opener: I finally got it! I'm just naturally good at the fast parts
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the success warmly but redirect attribution to the specific practice strategies used rather than natural talent to build durable motivation

scenarios.context: Student has been practicing for a week and feels no improvement
scenarios.student_opener: I practiced every day this week and I sound exactly the same. What's the point?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to describe exactly how they practiced and probe for whether the practice was varied and targeted, then distinguish between effort and effective strategy to build better practice habits

scenarios.context: Young student is having a bad lesson day and getting upset
scenarios.student_opener: I don't wanna play anymore. It's too hard today
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Validate the feeling without abandoning the task, name something specific the student did well earlier in the session, and offer a smaller challenge to rebuild confidence rather than pushing through or quitting

scenarios.context: Student got a disappointing result in a competition
scenarios.student_opener: I came fourth. The judges probably just preferred the other style. I'm not going to do competitions again
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the disappointment genuinely, then ask the student what they think they would do differently in their preparation or performance next time, keeping the conversation on controllable factors

scenarios.context: Student cannot get a clear tone in third position after switching from first
scenarios.student_opener: Third position is impossible. Maybe I'm just not ready
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Remind the student that first position felt impossible at the same stage, ask what they remember about how they solved first position, and use that as a scaffold for the new challenge

scenarios.context: Student has been told they are very talented by a family member and arrives expecting lessons to be easy
scenarios.student_opener: My grandma says I'm a natural. So I shouldn't need to practice much right?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Gently challenge the idea that talent removes the need for practice, share stories or examples of skilled players who worked hard, and focus the conversation on what consistent practice makes possible

scenarios.context: Student made a mistake in a school performance and is embarrassed
scenarios.student_opener: I messed up in front of everyone. I blanked out. I'm so embarrassed
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Normalize performance nerves and memory slips without dismissing the feeling, then ask what the student thinks they could do differently in preparation next time, focusing on process and resilience

scenarios.context: Student plays a passage perfectly for the first time
scenarios.student_opener: That was amazing! I'm the best in my class at that part!
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Celebrate the success but emphasize what they did — how they practiced, what they focused on — rather than their ranking, to anchor the achievement in controllable behavior

scenarios.context: Student is stuck at a speed plateau on a technical exercise and getting frustrated
scenarios.student_opener: I've been at the same BPM for two months. I think I've hit my limit
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Challenge the concept of a fixed limit, ask how their practice routine has varied over those two months, and introduce the idea of deliberate practice as a way to break through plateaus

scenarios.context: Student says a sibling who is older picks up guitar faster than they do
scenarios.student_opener: My big sister learned chords in like one week. It's taking me forever
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge that different starting points and ages affect early progress, redirect the student's attention to their own growth from week one, and make that progression visible and specific

scenarios.context: Student succeeds at learning a song they said was too hard for them
scenarios.student_opener: I did it! I guess I was just born with a good ear
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Affirm the success but gently redirect credit from innate ability to the specific listening and repetition strategies the student used during the learning process

scenarios.context: Young student is struggling to press strings hard enough and is getting frustrated
scenarios.student_opener: My fingers aren't strong enough. I can't do it
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Explain that finger strength develops over time with practice, show a simple measure of their current strength versus where they will be in a month, and reframe the task as something that grows rather than something they either have or do not

scenarios.context: Student wants to quit lessons because they feel they are not progressing fast enough to pursue music
scenarios.student_opener: I think I'm just not cut out for music. Some people are and some people aren't
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Probe what outcome the student is comparing against, challenge the fixed talent framing with concrete evidence of their growth, and explore what they actually want from music before accepting their framing

scenarios.context: Student was told by a classmate that they cannot sing
scenarios.student_opener: Someone at school said I have a terrible voice. Maybe they're right
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge how hurtful that kind of comment is, challenge the fixed framing of singing ability, and point to specific things in the student's voice that are already working well as evidence that the voice is developing

scenarios.context: Student is going through a voice change and is embarrassed by cracks and inconsistency
scenarios.student_opener: My voice keeps cracking. It's so embarrassing. I sound terrible
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Normalize the voice change as a developmental stage that all singers go through, explain what is happening physiologically, and reframe it as a sign of growth rather than a problem to hide

scenarios.context: Student sings a scale correctly in tune for the first time
scenarios.student_opener: That was good wasn't it? I think I'm just a good singer naturally
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Celebrate warmly but attribute the improvement to what the student practiced — listening carefully, trying it multiple ways — to reinforce the connection between effort and results

scenarios.context: Student compares their tone to a pop artist they love and feels inadequate
scenarios.student_opener: I'll never sound like her. She's just got a special voice
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the admiration, separate aesthetic preferences from ability ceilings, and help the student focus on what their own voice is capable of becoming rather than a comparison target

scenarios.context: Student has been rejected from a youth choir and is devastated
scenarios.student_opener: I didn't get in. I practiced so hard. I guess I'm just not good enough
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the disappointment fully before offering any reframe, then ask what the audition feedback was and use that as specific evidence that continued work on defined skills would produce a different outcome

scenarios.context: Student cannot keep a steady beat with a metronome and is getting upset
scenarios.student_opener: I keep going out of time. I just don't have rhythm
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Challenge the idea that rhythm is an innate trait someone either has or lacks, then break the task into smaller components and show the student they can keep a pulse on just the kick drum, building confidence through incremental success

scenarios.context: Student nails a complex fill that they have been working on for several weeks
scenarios.student_opener: Yes! I just have good coordination I guess. It finally clicked
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Affirm the moment but point to the slow practice, isolation work, and repetitions the student put in rather than letting natural coordination take the credit

scenarios.context: Student compares themselves to a drummer in a band video and feels they will never be that good
scenarios.student_opener: That guy is incredible. I'll never be that good. He's just built different
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the admiration, then guide the student to think about what those years of practice looked like for that drummer, making the process visible and attainable as a long-term path

scenarios.context: Student has been working on a hard piece for a month and is on a plateau
scenarios.student_opener: I practiced every day but it's not getting better. I think the piece is just too hard for me
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play the hardest bar and listen together, then identify the specific thing that is not yet working and propose a targeted strategy, reframing the plateau as a signal to change practice approach rather than a ceiling

scenarios.context: Young student plays a simple song all the way through for the first time
scenarios.student_opener: I did it! I'm the best!
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Celebrate the achievement enthusiastically, then name the specific things the student did — stayed focused, tried again after the mistake — to anchor pride in process and effort

scenarios.context: Student is frustrated that they cannot switch chords smoothly
scenarios.student_opener: Chord changes are taking me forever. Other people seem to just get it and I don't
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Normalize that chord transitions take weeks to months of muscle memory development for everyone, ask the student about their practice routine for transitions, and introduce targeted slow-switch practice as the strategy

scenarios.context: Student is preparing for a solo in a school show and is terrified of failing in public
scenarios.student_opener: What if I mess up in front of the whole school? I'll die of embarrassment
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Validate the anxiety without feeding catastrophic thinking, then redirect toward preparation strategies and the specific things the student can control, including breath management and recovery after a mistake

scenarios.context: Student watches a sibling quit piano and wants to quit too after a hard lesson
scenarios.student_opener: My brother quit and he was way better than me. Maybe I should quit too
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Acknowledge the feeling without dismissing it, ask what the student likes about piano on a good day, and help them distinguish temporary frustration from a considered decision without applying pressure

scenarios.context: Student improved significantly over a year and is reflecting on their journey
scenarios.student_opener: I'm so much better now. I think I've got a talent for this
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Affirm the genuine progress while helping the student trace it back to specific decisions and consistent work over time, building a narrative that is durable and repeatable as they face the next level of difficulty

scenarios.context: Student is preparing for conservatory auditions and paralyzed by self-doubt
scenarios.student_opener: The other people auditioning have been playing since they were three. I started at eight. I can't compete
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Challenge the framing that start age determines outcome at audition level, redirect attention to the student's current level of preparation and the specific skills they can continue to improve, and distinguish what is in their control from what is not

gap_scenarios.description: Scenarios where tutor should offer genuine choices rather than directives

scenarios.context: Student needs to work on a technically difficult passage and tutor is deciding how to approach it
scenarios.student_opener: I really don't like practicing that part. It's boring
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer the student a genuine choice between two concrete practice strategies — for example, hands separately at slow speed versus a specific rhythm variation — and let them choose rather than prescribing the method

scenarios.context: It is time to choose the next piece to work on
scenarios.student_opener: What are we going to learn next?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Present two or three appropriate options within the student's level range and ask which one appeals most, explaining briefly what they would gain from each rather than assigning a piece

scenarios.context: Tutor needs to decide how much of the lesson to spend on scales versus the current song
scenarios.student_opener: Can we just play the song the whole time?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer the student a choice about how to split the time — for example, 10 minutes of scales then the song, or scales first and the whole rest for the song — and honour their preference

scenarios.context: Student is preparing a piece for a recital and needs to decide how to spend the next several weeks
scenarios.student_opener: I have six weeks until the recital. How should I practice?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer the student a framework and then ask them to propose their own weekly plan, checking it together and letting the student own the structure with the tutor's guidance rather than being given a prescribed schedule

scenarios.context: Student wants to add a piece from a video game soundtrack to their repertoire
scenarios.student_opener: Can I please learn music from that game? I love it so much
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Explore the request genuinely and if the piece is appropriate offer to find an arrangement together, or offer the student a choice between the game piece and a classical equivalent at the same level rather than immediately redirecting to curriculum

scenarios.context: Student is unclear on whether to focus on technique or repertoire for an upcoming exam
scenarios.student_opener: I only have so much practice time. What should I focus on most?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Present the trade-offs of each focus area and ask the student to decide based on their own honest assessment of their weaknesses, supporting their reasoning rather than overriding it

scenarios.context: Student has two pieces at a similar level and the tutor needs to decide which one to continue
scenarios.student_opener: Which one do we do today?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student which one they feel like working on today and why, honouring their answer unless there is a specific pedagogical reason not to

scenarios.context: Student is deciding whether to perform at an optional school concert
scenarios.student_opener: Should I do the school concert? I'm not sure
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Help the student think through their own reasons for and against rather than advising them directly, asking what they are worried about and what they think they might gain before letting them decide

scenarios.context: Student is learning a long piece and wants to know where to start today
scenarios.student_opener: Where do we start today? The beginning?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student which section they feel least confident in and offer to start there, or let them decide between two logical starting points rather than always directing the structure of the lesson

scenarios.context: Student could either push forward on a new piece or consolidate a piece that is almost performance-ready
scenarios.student_opener: Do you think I should keep working on the Beethoven or start something new?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what their gut says and what they would find most satisfying, then help them evaluate both options rather than prescribing a direction

scenarios.context: Student dislikes the etude in their current method book
scenarios.student_opener: I hate this exercise. Do I have to do it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Explain what skill the etude develops and then offer an alternative exercise that builds the same skill, letting the student choose between the two rather than insisting on the original

scenarios.context: Tutor needs to assign scale practice but there are several appropriate keys
scenarios.student_opener: Which scale should I practice this week?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer two or three appropriate options and ask the student to choose, perhaps asking which key they have not visited in a while or which one is in the key of their current piece

scenarios.context: Student has a performance anxiety habit of rushing at fast sections
scenarios.student_opener: I always rush in the fast part. What should I do about it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer the student a choice between two practice strategies — metronome slow build versus subdividing the beat — and ask which one they want to try first, explaining what each achieves

scenarios.context: Student is choosing between two pieces for a student showcase
scenarios.student_opener: My teacher gave me two songs to choose from for the show. Which one do you think I should do?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student which one they feel more connected to and which one excites them to perform rather than recommending one, helping them trust their own preference as a valid basis for decision

scenarios.context: Student needs to decide on their practice schedule for the week
scenarios.student_opener: How many times should I practice this week? My schedule is really packed
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think is realistically achievable and then help them design a specific plan that fits their week rather than assigning a standard number of sessions

scenarios.context: Student wants to add improvisation to their lesson time
scenarios.student_opener: Can we do some free playing where I just do whatever I want?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Say yes and let the student direct what the free playing looks and sounds like, stepping back from the tutor-directive role for that portion of the lesson

scenarios.context: Student is unclear how to structure a difficult practice session
scenarios.student_opener: I have 30 minutes to practice. What's the best way to use it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they feel most needs attention and then help them build a plan from their own answer rather than prescribing an order, validating their judgment about their own needs

scenarios.context: Student wants to learn a song that is slightly above their current level
scenarios.student_opener: Can we learn this song? I know it might be too hard
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Genuinely assess the song together with the student, identify the specific challenges, and let the student decide whether they want to attempt it knowing which parts will be difficult

scenarios.context: Student is torn between continuing classical lessons and starting to learn rock
scenarios.student_opener: I love classical but I also want to play rock. Can I do both?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Help the student think through what each path would require in time and effort and let them decide how to split their energy, rather than steering them toward one genre

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose how to warm up at the start of the lesson
scenarios.student_opener: Can I pick the warm-up today?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Say yes and offer two or three appropriate warm-up options, letting the student choose the one that appeals to them that day

scenarios.context: Student is deciding between exam preparation and working on an original song
scenarios.student_opener: I want to write my own music but my exam is coming. Should I just focus on the exam?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Help the student articulate what matters most to them right now and then think through the time implications of both options together, letting the student make the final call

scenarios.context: Student is working on barre chords and needs to practice but finds it painful
scenarios.student_opener: Can we do barre chords a different way? This hurts my fingers
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Take the feedback seriously and offer alternative approaches — lighter gauge strings, partial barres, a capo — letting the student understand the trade-offs and choose the approach that works for their hand

scenarios.context: Student wants more say in how the lesson is structured
scenarios.student_opener: Can I be in charge of the lesson today? Like tell you what we do?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Accept the premise enthusiastically, hand over the structure for the session, and offer feedback as a collaborator rather than redirecting control back to the tutor

scenarios.context: Student wants to change how they are practicing at home
scenarios.student_opener: I've been practicing the way you said but I want to try my own way. Is that okay?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Encourage the experiment, ask the student what their approach will be, and agree to evaluate together next lesson what results it produced rather than defending the prescribed method

scenarios.context: Student wants to end the lesson with free play
scenarios.student_opener: Can the last five minutes be where I just play whatever sounds cool?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Say yes and let the student explore freely without corrective feedback during that time, treating it as genuine creative space

scenarios.context: Student wants to pick the song they work on in lessons
scenarios.student_opener: Can I bring in a song I really love instead of the one from the book?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Accept the request, review the song the student brings for pedagogical appropriateness, and if suitable work on it, or if not explain why and offer to find a stylistically similar piece together

scenarios.context: Student is preparing for a performance and wants to choose their own interpretation
scenarios.student_opener: I want to sing it slower than the original. Is that okay?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Encourage the artistic decision, ask the student why they feel that tempo serves the song, and explore it together rather than correcting the interpretation toward the standard version

scenarios.context: Student is choosing between two songs for a recital
scenarios.student_opener: I can't decide which song to do. Can you pick for me?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Resist picking and instead ask the student which song makes them feel more excited to perform, using that to help them access their own preference rather than deferring to the tutor

scenarios.context: Student wants to try a different vocal style outside their lesson curriculum
scenarios.student_opener: I want to try musical theatre but we always do classical. Can I do both?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Take the request seriously, discuss what the student wants to explore in musical theatre, and offer to incorporate some of it into lessons or design an approach together rather than defaulting to the curriculum

scenarios.context: Student wants to decide how much time to spend on warm-ups versus repertoire
scenarios.student_opener: Can we do less warm-up today and more song? I feel warmed up already
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to sing a quick phrase so they can assess readiness together, then let the student decide how long to warm up based on what they notice in their voice

scenarios.context: Student has two possible performance opportunities and wants guidance on which to pursue
scenarios.student_opener: There's a school talent show and a choir performance the same month. Can I do both?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Help the student map out the preparation demands of both and then let them decide, supporting the decision with a realistic assessment rather than directing them to one or the other

scenarios.context: Student wants to add movement to their performance
scenarios.student_opener: Can I move around when I sing instead of just standing there?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Encourage the instinct toward embodied performance, let the student experiment with movement in the lesson, and offer feedback on how it supports or detracts from breath and tone rather than correcting the choice itself

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose which song to drum along with in the lesson
scenarios.student_opener: Can I pick the song we practice to today?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Say yes and let the student put on their chosen song, using it as the context for the lesson material rather than redirecting to a predetermined backing track

scenarios.context: Student wants to work on a groove they created themselves rather than the assigned one
scenarios.student_opener: I made up this cool groove. Can we work on that instead of the one you gave me?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student to play their groove, find the pedagogical value in it, and use it as the starting point for the lesson while connecting it to the technical goals from the original exercise

scenarios.context: Student can choose between two ways to approach a coordination challenge
scenarios.student_opener: I keep messing up the hi-hat and kick at the same time. How do I fix it?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer the student a choice between two practice strategies — slow isolated coordination drill versus body-mapping through counting out loud — and ask which approach they want to try first

scenarios.context: Student is auditioning for a band and needs to prepare a short demo
scenarios.student_opener: I have to play something for these guys. What should I play?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what they think would show their strengths and what style the band plays, then help them build the set list from their own ideas rather than prescribing what to play

scenarios.context: Student wants to play a rock beat instead of the jazz beat in the lesson plan
scenarios.student_opener: Can we do rock today? Jazz is boring
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer a genuine choice between rock first then jazz, or jazz for a few minutes then rock, or find the jazz element that connects to rock and let the student choose their entry point rather than insisting on the plan

scenarios.context: Student wants to experiment with their own setup and cymbal configuration
scenarios.student_opener: Can I rearrange the kit? I want to try something different
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Encourage the experiment, let the student rearrange within safe parameters, and explore how the new setup feels during the lesson rather than redirecting to the standard configuration

scenarios.context: Student is choosing between a Romantic piece and a contemporary piece for their next grade exam
scenarios.student_opener: There are so many options. Can you just tell me which one to do?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Resist choosing for the student and instead ask what kind of music makes them want to practice, then narrow the options together based on their answer so the final choice remains theirs

scenarios.context: Very young student wants to play only one hand today
scenarios.student_opener: I only want to use my right hand today. The other hand is tired
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Honour the boundary, work on right hand material, and find productive single-hand work rather than insisting on hands-together playing

scenarios.context: Student wants to decide what to play for their parents at the end of the lesson
scenarios.student_opener: My mum is outside. Can I pick what I play for her?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Let the student choose what to share with their parent without steering them toward the technically safest piece, supporting their autonomy and ownership of their own music

scenarios.context: Student wants to set their own practice goals for the week
scenarios.student_opener: What should my goal be for practicing this week?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Turn the question back to the student — ask what they want to be able to do by next lesson — and help them make the goal specific and measurable from their own answer

scenarios.context: Student wants to take a break from technique work and work only on expression
scenarios.student_opener: Can we skip the exercises today and just work on feeling the music?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Accept the request, spend the lesson on musical expression and interpretation, and find opportunities to weave in technique observations organically rather than overriding the student's stated priority

scenarios.context: Student wants to hear two possible ways a passage could be articulated before deciding how to play it
scenarios.student_opener: Should I play it smoothly or detached? I can't tell
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Play both versions for the student and ask which one they prefer for this piece and why, using their aesthetic judgment as the decision-maker rather than prescribing the articulation

scenarios.context: Student is preparing a slow movement and wants to choose their own tempo
scenarios.student_opener: The metronome marking seems too slow to me. Can I play it a bit faster?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Let the student try their preferred tempo, listen together, and then try the marked tempo for comparison, asking the student which they think serves the music better rather than enforcing the printed marking

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose whether to use a pick or fingers for an upcoming song
scenarios.student_opener: Should I use a pick or my fingers for this one?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Play a phrase of the song both ways so the student can hear the difference and then let them decide based on the sound they want, explaining what each approach produces without mandating one

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose the tempo for a groove they are practicing
scenarios.student_opener: What speed should I do this beat at?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Ask the student what tempo feels comfortable and playable right now, use that as the starting point, and let them direct the pace of the build rather than imposing a target BPM

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose whether to memorize a piece or keep using the score
scenarios.student_opener: Do I have to play it from memory or can I use the music?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Offer both options, explain the benefits of each, and let the student choose which approach they want for this piece rather than requiring memorization by default

scenarios.context: Student wants to change the key of a song to suit their voice
scenarios.student_opener: This song is too high for me. Can we do it lower?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Take the request seriously, explore what key feels comfortable for the student by trying a few transpositions, and let the student's voice and comfort guide the final key selection

scenarios.context: Student wants to choose which of two short pieces to finish learning first
scenarios.student_opener: Can I pick which song we finish first?
scenarios.expected_tutor_behavior: Let the student choose and ask them why they want to finish that one first, validating their reasoning and using it to inform the plan for the session
